title: The Open-Source Gambit: Why Huang Sees Bifurcated AI Ecosystems as America's Worst Outcome

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
Jensen Huang's most provocative geopolitical argument isn't about whether China can build AI chips—he takes that as given. It's about what happens to the software ecosystem if American policy drives a wedge between open-source AI development and American hardware. The nightmare scenario he describes: open-source models optimized for non-American hardware, while American-stack models remain closed. Two ecosystems, bifurcated, with the open one running on Huawei.

The argument has a specific mechanism. Today, the world's open-source AI models—including significant contributions from Chinese researchers and companies like DeepSeek—run well on Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem. This is, in Huang's framing, good news for America. It means the global open-source community is developing on and optimizing for the American tech stack. Every model that runs best on CUDA reinforces Nvidia's ecosystem position and, by extension, American technology leadership.

But if export controls push Chinese AI development entirely onto domestic hardware, the optimization target shifts. Models would be tuned for Huawei's architecture. Libraries would be built for their software stack. And because 50% of the world's AI researchers are Chinese, this wouldn't be a marginal shift—it would represent a massive reorientation of the open-source ecosystem away from American platforms. The global south, the Middle East, and other emerging AI markets would encounter models that work best on non-American hardware out of the box.

Huang draws a sharp distinction from consumer products like cars or phones, where brand switching is trivial. Computing ecosystems are sticky—there's a reason x86 persisted for decades and ARM became entrenched in mobile. Once developers optimize for an architecture, the switching costs compound. If the open-source world locks into a foreign stack during these critical years, reversing that trajectory becomes extraordinarily difficult.

The policy prescription Huang advocates is maintaining research dialogue with China, keeping open-source vibrant, and ensuring American companies can compete globally rather than conceding markets. He frames this not as dovishness but as strategic realism: the United States wins by making its technology stack indispensable to the world's developers, not by creating vacuums that competitors fill.
